coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of biomass-derived carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Shuang
Gao, Ying
Ni, Binshan
Yang, Xiaodong
coredata.dc:description: Eco-friendly synthesis of Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in water is reported, exploiting various typical crop biomasses as carbon sources. The resultant CQDs exhibit strong fluorescence, good dispersion in common solvents and uniformly dispersed particle size. The synthesized CQDs are highly dispersible in water, due to the presence of surface hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The CQDs suspension exhibits strong blue fluorescence under UV irradiation, with an optimal excitation wavelength in the range from 350 to 390 nm, which varies with the change of CQD concentration. The emission corresponds to two band transitions, which are originated from π → π* of CC bonds and n → π* of CO bonds, respectively. Due to its strong fluorescence and energy transfer, the synthesized CQDs materials can be used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions with good selectivity and sensitivity, within the Fe3+ concentration range of 0–500 μM. The research is of significance for converting crop waste to high added-value products, and the obtained CQDs with selective Fe3+ ions determination ability are attractive for the environmental monitoring and medical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass conversion
Sensing
Carbon quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation procedure for CQDs from typical crop biomasses via hydrothermal treatment.

Photographic images of CQDs suspension from different starting materials and synthetic conditions, the top line is under visible light and the bottom line is under the UV irradiation with λmax at 365 nm..
XRD pattern of CQDs-C sample (from corn stalk, hydrothermally treated at 140 °C)

(a) TEM image; (b) The size distribution of fluorescence carbon dots; (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum and (d) infrared spectrum of the CQDs-C sample (from corn stalk, hydrothermally treated at 140 °C).
XPS analysis of as-prepared CQDs-C (from corn stalk, hydrothermally treated at 140 °C); (a) XPS survey spectrum; (b) High resolution spectrum of C1s.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs-C at different excitation wavelengths; (b) Effect of illumination on fluorescence intensity of CQDs-C; (c) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs; (d) Effect of NaCl concentration on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs, I0 is the PL intensity of CQDs BR buffer (pH = 7); I is the PL intensity of CQDs dispersion at diverse pH values;

(a) The fluorescence spectra of synthesized CQDs-C in the presence of different metal ions with a concentration of 500 μM (excitation wavelength of 350 nm); (b) The bar graph shows CQDs-C′ fluorescence intensity versus addition of different metal ions. (c) The fluorescence spectra of synthesized CQDs-C in the presence of Fe3+ ions with their concentration varied from 0 to 1000 μM (excitation wavelength of 350 nm); (d) Calibration graph for the Fe3+ ions with the CQDs-C system.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of different metal ions in the concentration of 200 μmol L−1.
The content of iron in iron supplements measured through the following two methods.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of biomass-derived carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin Engineering Normal University, Changchun 130052, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jilin Engineering Normal University, Changchun 130052, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chengde Petroleum College, 067000 Chengde, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chengde Petroleum College, 067000 Chengde, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The most abundant agricultural biomass in the Northeast of China can be directly converted into the carbon quantum dots. The obtained carbon quantum dots is able to detect iron(III) with high selectivity and good efficiency.

Eco-friendly synthesis of Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in water is reported, exploiting various typical crop biomasses as carbon sources. The resultant CQDs exhibit strong fluorescence, good dispersion in common solvents and uniformly dispersed particle size. The synthesized CQDs are highly dispersible in water, due to the presence of surface hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The CQDs suspension exhibits strong blue fluorescence under UV irradiation, with an optimal excitation wavelength in the range from 350 to 390 nm, which varies with the change of CQD concentration. The emission corresponds to two band transitions, which are originated from π → π* of CC bonds and n → π* of CO bonds, respectively. Due to its strong fluorescence and energy transfer, the synthesized CQDs materials can be used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions with good selectivity and sensitivity, within the Fe3+ concentration range of 0–500 μM. The research is of significance for converting crop waste to high added-value products, and the obtained CQDs with selective Fe3+ ions determination ability are attractive for the environmental monitoring and medical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are advanced carbon-based nanomaterials with their particle sizes below 10 nm. Since the pioneer research reported by Xu et al. [1] in 2004, CQDs have attracted extensive attention due to their unique advantages, such as high photoluminescence, good photo-induced charge transfer property, light resistance, high chemical stability, hydrophilicity and low toxicity. For their excellent performance, CQDs have been widely applied in chemical sensing [2–7], bio-sensing [8–10], bio-imaging [11,12], nanomedicine [13–15], photo-catalysis [16–18] and electro-catalysis [19,20], just to name a few [21–23]. The preparation of CQDs has become a hot research topic at the moment. Over the past years, the synthesis of CQDs has experienced explosive development. Various synthetic methods have been reported. According to the starting materials, these methods can be classified into two synthetic strategies, namely “top-down” and “bottom-up” synthesis. “Top-down” synthesis [24,25] refers to those CQDs obtained by stripping bulky carbon materials by physical or chemical treatments. The carbon sources for “top-down” synthesis of CQDs mainly include carbon nanotubes, carbon fibers, graphite rods, carbon ash and activated carbon. These carbon-rich materials were decomposed by arc discharge, laser ablation or electrochemical processing, leading to the formation of CQDs. In contrast to the “top-down” method, in “bottom-up” route [26,27], CQDs were synthesized from carbon-containing compounds such as small organic molecules or oligomers. The common carbon sources include citric acid, glucose, polyethylene glycol, urea, ionic liquids, and they can be treated by chemical oxidation, combustion, hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis, microwave synthesis, or templating method to afford CQDs. Among these treatments, the hydrothermal method is the most common one due to its low cost, environmentally-friendly and non-toxic features, which comply with the desires for green chemistry.

Biomass is renewable, naturally produced with large output, and considered as feedstocks for many organic compounds. However, the chemical conversion of biomass to high value-added product is relatively inadequate. Many biomass materials such as crop straws are usually burned directly, which is not only a waste of bio resources but also aggravates environmental pollution. Therefore, the efficient utilization of biomass has attracted significant attention. Considering their broad applications, direct synthesis of CQDs from biomass via the bottom up strategy has been reported recently [28–31]. For example, Mohapatra et al. successfully synthesized the CQDs with a size of 1.5–4.5 nm from orange juice and applied them in bioimaging [30]. Liu et al. reported a hydrothermal method synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent CQDs using chitosan as precursor treated at 180 °C for 12 h [28]. As one of the most common agricultural wastes, crop straws from corn, wheat and rice are highly output annually. Exploiting these raw materials as the feedstock of useful chemicals or materials would provide a new gateway for chemical and farming industry. As far as we know, there are few reports about the preparation of CQDs from typical crop wastes. Herein, a new type of CQDs material was prepared by hydrothermal method from typical crop wastes such as corn straw, wheat straw and rice straw. This is a combination of top-down and bottom strategies in one-pot, which brings the ease of operation and high synthetic efficiency to the preparation. Comprehensive characterizations were carried out to obtain their structural and fluorescent properties. Specifically, the fluorescence of CQDs is selectively quenched by the addition of Fe3+ ion in aqueous solution. Detecting of Fe3+ by CQDs holds the potential for the applications such as environmental remediation [32] and medical diagnosis [33].

The main components in typical crop biomass are biopolymers,such as cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin.They are rich in carbon, so they are very suitable as carbon sources to prepare CQDs. Meanwhile, there are abundant hydroxyl and ether groups in biomass molecules, rendering their water compatibility. Therefore, the biomasses are readily reacted in water. The synthetic procedure is demonstrated in Fig. 1 . Firstly, those biopolymers including cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin were hydrolyzed into saccharides and aromatic alcohols during the hydrothermal reaction process. This is a top-down strategy to give small molecules as precursors. Subsequently, the precursors undergo complex dehydration and condensation reactions, leading to the formation of CQDs. This is a bottom up strategy to afford carbon dots with uniform size. With the combination of both strategies, the biomass is readily converted to CQDs.

Significant Tyndall effect indicates the nano-sized particles exist in the CQD suspension (Fig. S1). Under visible light the CQD suspensions exhibit dark yellow color as shown in Fig. 2 . All the CQD suspensions synthesized at temperatures above 140 °C show darker color than those obtained at 120 °C, indicating the elevated temperature results in higher yield of CQDs and higher concentration in suspension. The color of the suspensions synthesized at 140 °C and 160 °C are similar. Therefore the optimal synthesis temperature is confirmed to be 140 °C. Strong blue fluoresce was observed when the suspensions were observed under UV irradiation with a λmax of 365 nm. Similarly, fluorescent intensity can be distinct from the suspension obtained at 120 °C and above 140 °C (Fig. 2).

The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the CQD suspensions synthesized at different conditions are shown in the supporting information (S2–S6). The strongest excitation wavelength of the synthesized CQDs is mainly concentrated at 370–390 nm, varied from the different carbon sources used. Compared with other CQDs synthesized from different starting materials and at different conditions, the CQDs-C synthesized from corn stalk at 140 °C shows the highest fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. For convenience, the characterizations and sensing properties of CQDs were all conducted based on the CQDs-C from 140 °C as a representative.

The XRD pattern of the CQDs-C was showed in Fig. 3 . A relatively broad peak around 21.3° corresponds to the (002) peak of CQDs. It also indicates the interlayer spacing (d) of the CQDs (0.42 nm) is larger than that of the graphitic interlayer spacing (0.33 nm) along with the broadness character [34].

The TEM image (Fig. 4 a) indicates the synthesized CQDs-C were well dispersed without aggregation. All the CQDs observed are spherical with average diameter of 2–5 nm. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements (Fig. 4b) show that the particle size distribution of the synthesized CQDs-C is mainly concentrated in 1.8–4.2 nm, which is consistent with the results of TEM. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs-C (diluted for 10 times) is shown in Fig. 4c. There are two absorption peaks appearing in the spectrum, one at 280 nm indicates the presence of π → π* transition of CC bond, and the other at 325 nm is attributed to the n → π* transition of CO bond. FT-IR spectrum indicates the presence of functional groups of CQDs-C as shown in Fig. 4d. The relatively broad absorption peaks at 3390, 3280 and 1405 cm−1 can be attributed to the OH vibrations, suggesting that there are abundant hydroxyl groups at CQD surface. The adsorption peak at 2928 cm−1 is assigned to the CH stretching vibration. The absorption peaks at 1717 and 1615 cm−1 indicate the presence of carboxyl (CO) functional groups. Moreover, the adsorption peak at 1093 cm−1 can be attributable to COC stretching vibration. The FTIR reveals that the synthesized CQDs-C are rich in carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl and other oxygen containing groups. The quantum yield of CQDs was 3.5% calculated by the method in the support information.

The XPS spectras of CQDs-C are shown in the Fig. 5 . The characteristic peaks of C and O appear at 284.6 and 532.3 eV (Fig. 5a), indicating that the sample mainly contains C and O elements. High resolution C1s spectra(Fig. 5b) show that there are three kinds of carbon in the sample, which are located at 284.6, 286.1, and 288.3 eV, corresponding to CCC, CO and CO bonds. It can be concluded that CQDs-C have a variety of polar groups including hydroxyl, alkyl and carboxyl groups.

Dilution of the CQDs-C suspensions results in the blue shifts of their optimal excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. S7, the strongest fluorescence intensity of the as-synthesized CQDs-C suspension was obtained with the excitation wavelength of 380 nm, indicating the optimal excitation wavelength for as-synthesized CQDs-C suspension is 380 nm. While the CQDs-C suspension was diluted by 10 and 100 times, the optimal excitation wavelength shifted to 370 and 350 nm, respectively. Finally, when the suspension was further diluted, the optimal excitation wavelength does not shift any more. Obviously the decrease of CQDs-C concentration causes the decrease of its fluorescence intensity and leads to the blue shift of its optimal excitation wavelength.

Fig. 6 a is the fluorescence spectrum of CQDs-C, showing that 380 nm is the best excitation wavelength. Fig. 6b shows that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-C fluctuates slightly with the increase of UV irradiation time, while the fluorescence intensity of fluorescein decreases sharply with the increase of irradiation time, indicating that the CQDs-C possesses good photostability. We also studied the effects of pH and high concentration salt on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-C in buffer solution and sodium chloride solution(Fig. 6c, d). Fig. 6c shows that the fluorescence intensity fluctuates smoothly when the pH range of the buffer solution is from 2 to 8, and decreases sharply with the increase of pH, indicating that the CQDs-C have good stability under acidic and neutral conditions, but instability in strong alkali solution. Fig. 6d shows that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-C does not change significantly with the increase of sodium chloride concentration, indicating that the CQDs-C can be preserved stably in high concentration salt solution.

The strong fluorescence of CQDs inspires that it may applicable as probe for metal ions. Quantitative detection of metals in a feasible way is widely attractive as they provide important information in environment or human healthy [29,30]. The application of CQDs suspension as fluorescent probe has been investigated. During the experiment, the CQDs-C solution was diluted 20 times, and the optimal excitation wavelength was 350 nm. Different metal ions including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Zn2+, K+ and Fe3+ aqueous solution at the concentration of 500 μM were mixed with buffer solution (pH = 7) and CQDs-C solutions, and the mixture was shaken at room temperature. Then, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs was recorded. Fig. 7 a showed the fluorescence spectra of synthesized CQDs -C in the presence of different metal ions with a concentration of 500 μM. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs suspension was significantly decreased by addition of Fe3+ ions, while the fluorescence intensities of CQDs with other metal ions are retained. Fig. 7b shows the relative intensity of original CQDs and their changes after the addition of metal ions. It is obvious that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs decreases by 40% with the addition of Fe3+, while the fluorescence intensities decrease by a maximum of 3.2% in the cases when other metal ions were added. Therefore, it can be concluded that the synthesized CQDs-C have significant selectivity for detection of Fe3+ over other metals. In view of the detection of Fe3+, we have carried out a more in-depth study to reveal its linear proportion of CQDs response to analytes, by adding Fe3+ ions solution in the concentration range from 0 to 1000 μM. Obviously, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-C decreases with the increased concentration of Fe3+ ions (Fig. 7c). As shown in Fig. 7d, we could clearly observed that the concentration of CQDs-C varied stepwise when the concentration of Fe3+ ions increased from 0 to 500 μM, and there was a good linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-C and the concentration of Fe3+ ions (R2 = 0.9993). The limit of detection concentration (LOD) was 5.23 μmol L-1, which was calculated according to the formula: LOD = 3σ/S (σ is the standard deviation of 11 blank samples, and S is the slope of the linear correlation). Therefore it is possible for the synthesized CQDs materials acting as an efficient fluorescent probe for the quantitative detection of Fe3+ ions with good selectivity and sensitivity. We also took iron supplements as analyte, and use CQDs-C as probes to test the iron concentration in the analyte. Simultaneously, we also compared with the traditional method of measuring iron content – o-phenanthroline spectrophotometry. The specific method can be seen in the support information. The results are shown in Table 1 . It can be seen from the Table 1 that the carbon quantum dot quenching method used in the paper is close to the traditional method. Compared with the traditional method, the carbon quantum dot quenching method is more environmentally friendly, simple to operate. Therefore, it has a good application prospect in practical application.

We also explored the mechanism of carbon quantum dots quenching for Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 8 . Fe3+ has a strong absorption at the wavelength of 250–375 nm, while other metal ions exhibit weak absorption peaks in the same range. Simultaneously, we found that carbon quantum dots also have strong absorption peaks during the wave band (Fig. 4c). The overlap between Fe3+ and CQDs is large, which leads to a competition between Fe3+ and CQDs for absorbing the light. The absorption light is the key factor for the luminescence of CQDs. The decrease of absorption light greatly weakens electron transition from valence band to conduction band. Correspondingly, this leads to the fluorescence quenching of CQDs, which confirms that the fluorescence quenching mechanism for sensing Fe3+ is an absorption competition quenching (ACQ) mechanism[35,36].

In summary, we have reported a simple and environmental friendly method in producing CQDs materials from typical crop wastes. The biomass derived CQDs exhibited significant photoluminescence, good homogeneity and water compatibility. The PL spectra showed that the optimum excitation wavelength of the synthesized CQDs materials is between 350 and 390 nm. The XRD and TEM results proved that the synthesized CQDs materials possess a narrow size distribution at 2–5 nm. The UV–Vis spectrum clearly suggested the presence of π → π* and n → π* transition in the synthesized CQDs materials. FTIR spectra indicated the synthesized CQDs are rich of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Moreover, the synthesized CQDs materials could be used as an efficient fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions with good selectivity and sensitivity during the concentration range of 0–500 μM. It is found that the application of carbon quantum dots in the determination of iron in iron supplements is feasible. Compared with the traditional method, the carbon quantum dot quenching method is more environmentally friendly and convenient. We believe that our research will provide new alternative for the usages of crop wastes and the quantitative detection and identification of Fe3+ ions for environmental science and human health.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51875249), Jilin Engineering Normal University PhD startup foundation (No. BSKJ201829) and Jilin Engineering Normal University general intramural foundation (No. XYB201822).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2021.108636.

ce:section.ce:para:
